title: SEGMENT 2 — The conventional wisdom: differentiation is one-way

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The conventional wisdom: differentiation is one-way
For most of the twentieth century, developmental biology operated under a set of intuitions that were so deeply held they were rarely even articulated.
They were just the background assumptions, the water the field swam in.
Let me lay them out for you, because Yamanaka's work is, in essence, a frontal assault on every one of them.
Assumption one: differentiation is one-way.
An adult cell — a skin fibroblast, a liver hepatocyte, a cardiac myocyte — has a fixed identity.
You can lesion it, you can starve it, you can put it in any culture medium you like, and it will either die or stay what it is.
It won't, of its own accord, decide to become something else.
The cell's identity, once committed, was thought to be locked.
Assumption two: the embryo is the only source of pluripotency.
The pluripotent state — the state where a cell can become any tissue in the body — exists only for a brief window in the early embryo.
After about day six of human development, when the inner cell mass differentiates and starts forming the three germ layers — ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm — pluripotency is gone.
The cells become multipotent at best, restricted to lineages within their germ layer.
By the time you're an adult, the only stem cells in your body are tissue-specific adult stem cells with very limited capabilities.
Hematopoietic stem cells in your bone marrow can make blood, but not liver.
Intestinal stem cells can make gut, but not neurons.
The fully pluripotent state — the ability to make anything — is over by the second week of life.
It's gone.
You can't get it back.
Assumption three: gene expression in adult cells is essentially permanent.
Once a cell has settled into being, say, a liver cell, the network of transcription factors maintaining its liver identity is self-reinforcing.
The liver-specific genes are on, the alternative-lineage genes are silenced — packed into tight heterochromatin, marked with repressive methyl groups, locked away.
To switch a liver cell to a neuron, you'd have to undo all of that silencing and impose a new pattern.
It's not impossible in principle, but the architecture is so deeply baked in that nobody really expected it could be done with simple molecular tools.
There were hints, even then, that the picture might not be quite that clean.
Some of the hints came from natural biology.
Salamanders, famously, can regenerate amputated limbs.
The cells at the wound site dedifferentiate — they lose their adult identity, become a kind of stem cell called a blastema, and then redifferentiate into all the tissues of a new limb.
So at least in salamanders, the developmental tape clearly could be partially rewound.
And some hints came from experiments.

As early as 1958, a developmental biologist named Frederick Steward took a fully differentiated carrot root cell and showed that, under the right conditions, it could grow back into an entire carrot plant.
Differentiation was clearly reversible in plants.
Whether it was reversible in animals — particularly in mammals — was a much more contested question.

But the dominant intuition in mammalian biology, the textbook view, the assumption underneath every grant proposal and every lecture, was that differentiation was, for all practical purposes, irreversible.
Adult cells stay adult.
That was the consensus.
And then John Gurdon, in 1962, decided to test it.
The conceptual frame I want you to hold onto for the rest of this lecture is this: in any complex system, the information is one thing, and the state is another.
The information in a cell is its DNA — the full blueprint, every gene, every regulatory element.
Every cell in your body, with a few exceptions, has the same DNA.

The state of a cell, on the other hand, is which subset of that information is being actively used — which genes are on, which are off, which transcription factors are bound to which regulatory regions, what the chromatin looks like, what proteins are floating around.
Differentiation is, fundamentally, a change in state, not a change in information.
The skin cell has the same DNA as the egg it came from.
It just has a different program loaded onto that DNA.
If that's true — if the DNA is the same and only the state differs — then in principle, you should be able to change the state without changing the DNA.
You should be able to load a different program.
You should be able to take a skin cell and convince it, by changing which transcription factors are around, to become an embryonic stem cell, or even a neuron.
The conventional wisdom said this couldn't be done.
The conceptual logic said it should be possible.
Yamanaka's work — and Gurdon's before him — was the experimental resolution of that tension.
Let's go meet Gurdon.
